advantages: Gives a unified geometric picture linking loss values, parameters, and optimizer behavior in one mental model.
Explains practical training phenomena—plateaus, divergent steps, sensitivity to initialization—without treating them as random bugs.
2D slices and contour plots make high-dimensional optimization intuition accessible to beginners.
Connects optimization to generalization via minimum sharpness/flatness, bridging training loss and test performance.
Clarifies when convex guarantees apply (unique global minimum) versus when heuristics dominate in non-convex deep learning.
disadvantages: Low-dimensional drawings can mislead: saddles and minima behave differently in very high dimensions than in 2D sketches.
The full loss surface for modern models is impossible to visualize or enumerate; slices may not represent the region optimizers actually visit.
Landscape metaphors risk over-emphasizing local minima as the main training obstacle when saddles, curvature, and noise often matter more.
Computing dense surface grids does not scale; exhaustive plotting is limited to toy models and tiny parameter subsets.
Flat-minima/generalization stories are informative but not universal laws—architecture, data, and regularization still dominate outcomes.
